content.full_text:
Fern PolrrrcAl Pnecrrcss CoMMrssroN
428 J Street r Suire 620 o Sacramento, CA g5gl1_2329
(916) 322-5660 o Fax (9t6) 322_0886
May 25,2010
Jared Huffman
Assembly Member
State Capitol
P. O. Box 942849
Sacramento, California 94249 -0006
RE: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-10-078
Dear Assemblyman Huffman:
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the behested payment
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the ..Act").1
QUESTION
Does Section 82015(bX2XBXiii) require a member of the State Assembly to report, as
"behested payments," grants made by a nonprofit 501(c)(3) organization (the ,.Funding
Organization") to another nonprofit 501(cX3) organization (the "Recipient") if, prior to the
qranJs, the assembly member sends to the Funding Organization a letter of suppo.t that vouches
for the Recipient's worthiness as a recipient of the gruitr?
CONCLUSION
Yes' Under Section 82015(b)(2)(Bxiii) and 18225.7, thegrants paid to the Recipienr are
payments made at the behest of the assembly member and must, iherefoie, be reported under
Section 82015(b)(2XBXiii) if they, or other payments made by rhe Fundin gor-ganizarion at your
behest during the calendar year, total g5,000 oi,no.".
FACTS
You are a member of the California State Assembly. You wish to assist the Recipient, a
local nonprofit organization in your district, with its fundraising efforts. Specifically, you woulcl
like to assist the Recipient in obtaining grants from the Fundin! organization by sending a letter
I The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections g1000 through gl0l4, All statuklry
ref'erences are to the Government Code' unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair political practices
commission are contained in Sections l8l l0 through 18997 of ritle 2 of thi Califirrnia code of Regulations. All
regulatory ref'erences are to Title 2, Division 6 of thi Califbrnia Code of Regulations. unless.therwise indicated.


File No. A-10-078
Page No. 2
of support that vouche_s for the Recipient's worthiness as a recipient of the grant. you are
concerned that under Section 82015(bX2XB)(iii), you may be rlquired to fiie a ,.behesred
payment report" should the Recipient succeed in obtaining a grani of $5,000 or more from the
Funding organization. You suggest that because the payients would flow from one nonprofit
organization to another, there is no potential for influencing you, particularly in view of the fact
that, under federal tax law governing nonprofit 501(c)(3) oiganizations, the Funding
organization is strictly prohibited from participating in, or iitervening in, ony political campaign
on behalf of' or in opposition to, a candidate for puUtic office. you believe that treatment of
these payments as "behested payments" would shed no light on the Act's .,core concern,,
regarding the influence gained by generous donors and initead would likely give the public the
misleading impression that the organizations are making campaign donationJor are otherwise
involved in other prohibited activities. For these reason-s, you believe that funds granted to the
Recipient by the Funding organization are not reportable ibehested payments,,;
AIIALYSIS
The Act states that when a payment is made at the behest of an elected official for a
legislative, govemmental, or charitable purpose and the payment is not otherwise a gift or
contribution, the official must report the payment if, when combined with similar piyments by
the same source during a calendar year, the total of the payments is $5,000 or more. (Section
82015(b)(2XBXiii).) Generally, a grant is made at youi ulhest if you solicited, requested, or
suggested the grant, or the grant is otherwise made io the recipieni with your cooperation,
consultation, coordination, or consent. (see Regul ation lgz2i.7(a).)
Regulation 18225.7 defines when a payment is "made at the behest of, a person and
subdivision (d) of that regulation creates a "safe harbor" for payments that meet any one of seven
criteria' The only provision we believe may be applicable toyour facts is subdivision (dX4),
which provides that an expenditure is not made oi irr" behest of a candidate or committee merely
when the person making the expenditure is responding to a general, non-specific request for
support by a candidate or committee, providedthere ii no discussion with the candidate or
committee prior to the expenditure relating to details of the expenditure. tn interpreting the
meaning of a "general, non-specific request for support," we have reviewed the minutes of the
Commission meeting at which this regulation was-atopted. From the commissioners,
discussions, it is clear that in adopting this language, the csmmission intended that this safe
harbor should not be used to avoid diiclosure or p-ayments resulting from one-on-one
communications, such as a "specific request to a person or group."- (See page six of the minutes
of the Commission's March 7,2004 meeting, comments or-cnuir*un ceim"an). Altemative
Ianguage, "public request for support", was iejected in favor of " generctl or non-specificrequest
for support" to ensure that payments resulting from one-on-one communications would be
reported' You indicate that your letter of support will be sent to a specific entity in support of a
specific grant. The plain language of the regulation, together with the history of its adoption,
makes it clear that this letter is not a "gene.il" or,.non-ipecific,, request r-,uppon.
We next address your argument that funds granted by the Funding Organization to the
Recipient are not behested payments because there is no potential for inf'luence over an official


File No. A-10-078
Page No. 3
inasmuch as federal tax law strictly prohibits 501(cX3) nonprofit organizations from
participating in, or. intervening in, any political 
"urrpuign o, u.norif, or in opposition to, a
candidate for public office. You also state that disciosire of the grant is likdy to give the public
the misleading impression that "instead of awarding grants funds to fellow nJnprofits, the
organizations are making campaign donations or ur" itherwise involved in other prohibited
activities."
We find your argument unpersuasive. It ignores the fact that, by its very^terms, Section
82015(b)(2)(BXiii) requires the reporting of paynients thar are not conrributions.. Hence,
reporting the grants does not give the impresiion that the Funding Organization is making
campaign donations. Such an implication is not supported by the-staiutory tanguage.
You also suggest that the potential for influence over an official is lacking where an
elected official write.s a letter of support for a nonprofit 501(c)( 3) organization seeking a grant
from another 501(c)(3) organization. rnt argument presupposes that the activities of a nonprofit
501(c)(3) organization would never be the.subject of iegistuiion on which a legislator might vote
and that such an organization could never uenirit from lurrying favor with a lEgrslator. That is
simply not the case. This is recognized in the Act's conflici-of-interest rules, which expressly
include nonprofit entities as source-of-income economic interests that could give rise to a
conflict of interesr. (Regulation 1g705.3(bX2).)
In addition, in prior advice we have concluded that even where a grant application must
be accompanied by a letter of recommendation of a legislator, the legislaior must file a behested
payment report. (Romero Advice Letter, No. I_09_044.)
For the foregoing reasons, we conclude that, under section g2015(bx2)(Bxiii), you must
report, as "behested payments," grants meeting the annual dollar threshold ;il; 
made by the
Funding organization to the Recipient if, prioito the grant, you send a letter of support vouching
for the Recipient's worthiness as i recipient of th" grilt.
If you have other questions on this matter, please conract me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
General
By: Valentina Joyce
Counsel, Legal Division
VJjgl
2 Section 82015, which not only defines "contribution" and imposes the reporting requirement of behested
payments' creates a presu.mption.that a payment is not acontribution if it is "made uy o ,u,iffit organization that is
exempt fiom taxation under Section 501(cX3) of the Internal Revenue Code," or it is ..principally tbr legislative,
governmental, or charitable purposes, itt w'hiclt case it is neitlrcr a gift nor a contribution.,' (Emphasis added).
